Agents don't fail loudly. They fail politely — a confident wrong answer at step 7 of 9 that nobody sees until Friday. The fix is designed, not prompted. Here's what a survivable agent spec includes.

First: allowed actions, written as a whitelist. Not "be careful with the database" — a list of tools it may call and files or records it may touch. Everything else denied by default. Vague permissions are how demos become incidents.

Second: evidence requirements. Every action returns proof — the query it ran, the rows it read, the source it cited. Without evidence you're not reviewing the agent's work. You're reviewing its self-esteem.

Third: the stop condition, the most-skipped part. Name the exact states where the agent halts and asks: input missing, confidence low, action irreversible, cost above N. An agent without a stop condition doesn't have autonomy. It has momentum.

Fourth: the handoff. When it stops, what does the human receive? A good handoff: what I tried, what I found, what's blocking me, what I recommend. A bad handoff: 47 log lines and a shrug. Design the handoff like a UI, because it is one.

Then run the first 20 executions with a named reviewer and count interventions. Under 3 in 20 with clean handoffs — expand scope. More than that, shrink the task. Autonomy is earned in production, not granted in the system prompt.
